meta.title: CSS, Tailwind & Styling
meta.description: CSS, Tailwind CSS, CSS-in-JS, responsive design, and styling architecture

items.text: Are specificity conflicts avoided (no !important, no overly specific selectors)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Keep selector specificity low and consistent. Avoid !important except in utility classes. Don't chain IDs or deeply nest selectors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Specificity wars make CSS unmaintainable. Once !important is used, it escalates until styles are impossible to override.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for !important usage. Check for selectors with more than 3 levels of nesting. Look for ID selectors used for styling.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses !important to override a style. Fix the specificity issue at the source instead of escalating.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep specificity flat. Use classes, not IDs. Reserve !important for utility overrides only.

items.text: Do class names follow a consistent convention (BEM, utility-first, CSS Modules)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use a consistent naming methodology: BEM (.block__element--modifier), utility-first (Tailwind), or CSS Modules (scoped by default).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent naming leads to style conflicts, difficulty finding styles, and unclear relationships between elements.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that new classes follow the project's established convention. Don't mix BEM with ad-hoc names.
baseContent.exampleComment: The project uses BEM but this component uses generic class names like .title and .container. Use .card__title and .card__container.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow the project's naming convention. Consistency matters more than which convention you choose.

items.text: Are magic numbers replaced with design tokens or CSS custom properties?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Hardcoded values like 'padding: 13px' or 'color: #3a7bc8' should use CSS custom properties (--spacing-md) or design tokens.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Magic numbers make themes impossible, break consistency, and are hard to update globally. A design system needs centralized values.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for hardcoded pixel values, hex colors, and font sizes that don't reference custom properties or theme tokens.
baseContent.exampleComment: padding: 13px is a magic number. Use var(--spacing-md) or the design system's spacing scale.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use design tokens and CSS custom properties. Avoid hardcoded values for spacing, colors, and typography.

items.text: Are appropriate CSS units used (rem/em for text, relative units for layout)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use rem for font sizes (respects user preferences), px for borders, and relative units (%, vw, vh, fr) for layout.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using px for font sizes ignores user accessibility settings. Using fixed units for layout breaks on different screen sizes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check font-size declarations for px units. Verify layout uses relative units where appropriate.
baseContent.exampleComment: font-size: 14px ignores user font size preferences. Use font-size: 0.875rem instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use rem for typography, relative units for layout. Reserve px for borders and fine details.

items.text: Is the design mobile-first (min-width breakpoints, not max-width)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Write base styles for mobile, then add complexity with min-width media queries. This is the standard responsive approach.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mobile-first means mobile users get simple CSS. Desktop users get additional rules. Max-width (desktop-first) ships bloated CSS to mobile.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for max-width media queries (sign of desktop-first). Verify base styles work on mobile without media queries.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses max-width: 768px to hide on mobile. Use mobile-first: show by default, hide with min-width: 769px.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write mobile styles first. Enhance for larger screens with min-width media queries.

items.text: Are breakpoints consistent and from a design system?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use consistent breakpoints from your design system or Tailwind config. Don't use arbitrary values like @media (min-width: 847px).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Arbitrary breakpoints create inconsistencies. Content shifts at different widths in different components.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check media queries for non-standard breakpoint values. Verify they match the design system or Tailwind config.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses min-width: 847px but the design system breakpoints are 640/768/1024/1280. Use the nearest standard breakpoint.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use design system breakpoints consistently. Don't create one-off breakpoint values.

items.text: Is horizontal scroll prevented (no overflow on mobile)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Content should not cause horizontal scrolling on any viewport. Fixed-width elements, wide tables, and images can break layout.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Horizontal scroll is a broken experience on mobile. Users can't see content and may not realize they can scroll sideways.
baseContent.howToVerify: Test on mobile viewport sizes. Check for fixed-width elements, images without max-width: 100%, and tables without responsive handling.
baseContent.exampleComment: This image has width: 800px which causes horizontal scroll on mobile. Use max-width: 100% instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prevent horizontal overflow. Use max-width: 100% on images and responsive patterns for tables.

items.text: Are interactive elements large enough for touch (44x44px minimum)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Buttons, links, and interactive elements should have a minimum touch target of 44x44 CSS pixels (WCAG 2.5.5).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Small touch targets cause misclicks on mobile. Users with motor impairments struggle with targets under 44px.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check button and link sizes on mobile. Verify padding creates adequate touch targets even if text is small.
baseContent.exampleComment: This icon button is 24x24px. Add padding to make the touch target at least 44x44px.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Make touch targets at least 44x44px. Use padding to increase hit area without changing visual size.

items.text: Are arbitrary values ([13px], [#abc]) avoided in favor of the design system scale?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tailwind's arbitrary value syntax (w-[137px]) bypasses the design system. Use the predefined scale (w-32, w-36) or extend the theme.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Arbitrary values defeat the purpose of a design system. They create one-off values that break consistency.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for square bracket syntax in class names. Each should be justified or replaced with a theme value.
baseContent.exampleComment: p-[13px] is an arbitrary value. Use p-3 (12px) or p-3.5 (14px) from the spacing scale, or extend the theme.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the design system scale. Extend the Tailwind config for project-specific values instead of arbitrary values.

items.text: Are repeated utility combinations extracted into components or @apply?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When the same utility combination appears in multiple places, extract it into a component or use @apply in a CSS file.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated utility strings are hard to maintain. Changing the design requires finding and updating every instance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for identical long class strings repeated across files. These should be components or @apply abstractions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This exact set of 12 utility classes appears in 5 files. Extract it into a <Button> component.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Extract repeated utility combinations into components. Use @apply sparingly for non-component abstractions.

items.text: Are responsive modifiers (sm:, md:, lg:) used correctly and mobile-first?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tailwind is mobile-first. Base utilities apply to all sizes. sm:, md:, lg: add styles at increasing breakpoints.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using lg: without base styles means large screens get styles but mobile is broken. Always start with mobile styles.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that elements have base styles (no prefix) for mobile. Responsive prefixes should add or override for larger screens.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses md:flex but has no display set for mobile. Add hidden as base and md:flex to show on desktop.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Start with mobile styles (no prefix). Add responsive modifiers for larger screens.

items.text: Are dark mode styles properly implemented with dark: variant?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Tailwind's dark: variant for dark mode styles. Ensure all custom colors have dark mode equivalents.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing dark mode styles cause white backgrounds or invisible text in dark mode, making the UI unusable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new components for dark: variants on backgrounds, text colors, and borders. Test in dark mode.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component uses bg-white but has no dark:bg-gray-800. Users in dark mode will see a blinding white card.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add dark: variants for all color-related utilities. Test in both light and dark modes.

items.text: Do text colors meet WCAG contrast requirements (4.5:1 for normal text)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Text must have sufficient contrast against its background. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Low contrast text is unreadable for users with low vision, in bright sunlight, or on low-quality displays.
baseContent.howToVerify: Use a contrast checker tool. Check light gray text on white backgrounds and dark text on dark backgrounds.
baseContent.exampleComment: text-gray-400 on bg-white has a contrast ratio of 3.0:1, below the 4.5:1 minimum. Use text-gray-600 or darker.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check contrast ratios. Use WCAG AA (4.5:1) as the minimum for all normal text.

items.text: Are focus styles visible for keyboard navigation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Interactive elements must show a visible focus indicator when navigated with keyboard. Don't remove outline without a replacement.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Removing focus styles makes the site unusable for keyboard users. They can't tell which element is focused.
baseContent.howToVerify: Tab through the interface. Every interactive element should show a visible focus ring. Check for outline: none without a replacement.
baseContent.exampleComment: This button has outline: none but no custom focus style. Add a visible focus ring: focus:ring-2 focus:ring-blue-500.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never remove focus styles without a replacement. Use focus-visible for mouse-friendly focus styles.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Animations should be disabled or simplified for users who set prefers-reduced-motion in their OS settings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Animations can cause motion sickness, seizures, or distraction for users with vestibular disorders.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for animations and transitions. Verify they use @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) to disable or simplify.
baseContent.exampleComment: This spinning animation has no reduced-motion alternative. Add motion-reduce:animate-none in Tailwind.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Respect prefers-reduced-motion. Provide static alternatives for all animations.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Elements should not shift position after the page loads. Set explicit dimensions on images/video. Use skeleton loaders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Layout shifts cause users to click the wrong element. They're frustrating and hurt Core Web Vitals scores.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for images without width/height. Look for dynamically injected content that pushes existing content down.
baseContent.exampleComment: This image has no width/height set. When it loads, it pushes content down. Add explicit dimensions or use aspect-ratio.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set explicit dimensions on media. Use skeleton loaders for dynamic content.

items.text: Are styles properly scoped (CSS Modules, scoped styles, or utility classes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Styles should be scoped to their component to prevent leaking. Use CSS Modules, styled-components, or utility classes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Global CSS leaks cause unexpected style changes in unrelated components. This is the #1 CSS maintenance problem.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for global CSS that could affect other components. Verify new styles are scoped to their component.
baseContent.exampleComment: This global .title class could affect other components. Use CSS Modules (.module.css) or a more specific selector.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope styles to components. Use CSS Modules, utility classes, or CSS-in-JS to prevent leaking.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Remove CSS that's no longer used by any component. Use PurgeCSS or Tailwind's built-in purging to eliminate unused utilities.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unused CSS increases bundle size and download time. Over time, CSS files grow without limit if unused styles aren't removed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for CSS classes that don't match any HTML/JSX element. Verify Tailwind purge configuration covers all template files.
baseContent.exampleComment: These 50 lines of CSS for the old header are no longer used after the redesign. Remove them.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Remove unused CSS. Configure purging for utility-first frameworks.
